content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
February 2, 1998
Jean B. Savaree
City Attorney
City ofBelmont
1070 Sixth Avenue
Belmont, California 94002-3 893
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-97-577
Dear Ms. Savaree:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Because you have not disclosed the identity of the
councilmembers for whom you seek this advice, we would ordinarily refuse to provide advice of
any kind. However, because you do include a letter from councihnember Coralin Feierbach and
further because we believe some general advice may be instructive to you with regard to future
requests, we have chosen to respond but are treating this as a request for informal assistance,
under Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed).
2
As briefly mentioned above, you have included in the materials forwarded to this office a
separate letter from Ms. Feierbach to the Fair Political Practices Commission (“FPPC”) seeking a
response regarding conflict issues in this matter; you state in your letter that
Ms. Feierbach’s letter is “part of this request for a written opinion.” Therefore, we will comply
with your instruction and treat Ms. Feierbach’s letter as information supporting your request and
not as a separate request for advice.
3
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 1 8329(c)(3).) Due to the fact that you have included a letter authored
by Councihnember Feierbach, we assume that she is one of the three councilmembers on whose behalf you write.
However, because you have neither specifically referenced her nor any other member of the city council, we
provide informal, general assistance only. (See Regulation 1 8329(c)(4)(C).)
The inclusion of Ms. Feierbach’s letter does not change our decision to render informal assistance only.
As will be explained under the General Conclusions portion of this letter, we have not received sufficient
428 J Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, California 95814 (916) 322-5660


File No. 1-97-577
Page 2
Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed as evaluation of any
conduct which may already have taken place.
4 Further, this letter is based on the facts as they
have been presented to us. The Conunission does not act as the finder of fact in providing
advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTIONS
1. May a councilmember who lives within 300 feet of the properties that are subject to
the advisory measure, participate in decisions to approve funding to implement the financial
plan?
5
2. May a councilmember who lives within 2,500 feet of the properties that are subject to
the advisory measure, participate in decisions to approve funding to implement the financial
plan?
3. May a councilmember who is a real estate agent and who has represented buyers and
sellers ofproperty located within the San Juan Canyon, participate in decisions to approve
funding to implement the financial plan?
information from any source to warrant a formal advice analysis. As an additional problem, Ms. Feierbach
references a Ms. Rianda in her letter and purports to seek advice on her behalf Unfortunately, we are not told who
Ms. Rianda is nor does Ms. Feierbach appear to have authority to seek advice, either formal or informal, for her.
(See Regulation 18329.)
You mention in your letter that last summer the Belmont City Council considered and declined to
approve a proposed ordinance seeking a moratorium of development of the San Juan Canyon. Because the city
council has already acted on that matter, we will not evaluate the propriety of any councilmember’s action with
respect to the matter as it constitutes past conduct under Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed). Ms. Feierbach seems to
imply in her letter that the moratorium ordinance may be resurrected. If that occurs, any affected councilmember
may request advice from this office with respect to prospective conduct to be taken in the decisionmaking process.
Generally speaking, however, any councilmember will be disqualified from participating in the consideration of the
ordinance if the decision will have a foreseeable and material impact on a fmancial interest of the coundilmember.
(Section 87103.)
You state in your letter that the reason you are contacting the FPPC is to obtain a written opinion (which
we have construed to mean a written advice letter) on behalf of certain councilmembers who must vote to “approve
the expenditure of funds to implementthe [financial] plan” required by th4assage of Measure E (the advisory
measure). We understand this to be the precise question at issue although later in your letter you appear to expand
the question to include the ability of the councilmembers to participate in “any decision which would be necessary
in order to move forward with ... the ultimate acquisition of land ....“ Because no proposal has yet been
developed to accomplish the actual acquisition of the San Juan Canyon land (apparently the completion of the
fmancial plan is preparatory to any plan of acquisition), it is impossible to evaluate whether a particular
councilmember would have a conflict of interest with respect to acquisition matters. Accordingly, we have phrased
your questions as posing the limited inquiry of whether a councilmember may participate in the vote on the
expenditure of funds for the fmancial plan.


File No. 1-97-577
Page 3
FACTS
Last summer the City Council ofthe City ofBelmont considered two separate items
which relate to the San Juan Canyon in the City of Belmont. First, the council considered a draft
moratorium ordinance regarding development of the Canyon. Three of the five councilmembers
indicated they were “conflicted” and recused themselves from the vote on the draft ordinance.
Because consideration of the draft ordinance was an urgency measure, four councilmembers
were needed to consider the matter. Per the rule ofnecessity, one councilmember was brought
back by the drawing of lots. The draft moratorium ordinance was not adopted by the city
council.
The council also considered an advisory measure for the November ballot asking whether
a financial plan should be developed to purchase the Canyon for open space and park purposes.
6
That advisory measure was acted on affirmatively by the council and appeared on the November
1997 ballot (Measure B).
The advisory measure was adopted by the voters and the city will now need to vote to
approve the expenditure of funds to implement the financial plan.
One councilmember lives within 300 feet of the properties which are subject to the
advisory measure, one councilmember lives within 2,500 feet of the properties, and one
councilmember is a real estate agent who, in the past, has represented buyers and sellers of
property located within the San Juan Canyon.
7
6 We were not provided the actual text of the advisory measure but take the explanation of the measure
from the memorandum prepared by the city attorney’s office. The absence of the text of the advisory measure and
any specffics regarding the frnancial plan to be implemented by the city council raise a threshold problem for this
analysis. The Commission has previously determined that once a basic policy decision has been reached (by vote of
the governing body or by approval of some other kind of directive such as an ordinance, ballot measure or
legislative proposal) officials may participate - without regard to conflicts - in subsequent decisions which
implement, but do not change the underlying policy decision. (See At/ian Advice Letter, No. A-86-094; Strauss
Advice Letter, No. A-96-034; and Talley Advice Letter, No. A-96-123.) Without further information, we cannot
determine if implementation of the financial plan would fall within the abo4e “exception” to the conflicts rules. For
purposes ofresponding to your request, we are assuming that the city council’s consideration of the fmancial plan is
not merely an implementing procedure.
Your letter and supporting materials reference “properties subject to the advisory measure,” “the San
Juan Canyon,” and “parcels to be included within the defmition of the San Juan Canyon” without any apparent
distinction despite the differences in names. Therefore, for purposes of your questions and this letter, we will
assume all of these properties are one in the same and will be subject to the city council’s decisions concerning the
fmancial plan. Hereinafter, we will refer to these properties as “the project.”


File No. 1-97-577
Page 4
GENERAL CONCLUSIONS
Comments applicable to all questions
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in, or using their
official position to influence a governmental decision in which they know or have reason to
know they have a financial interest. An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official or on a
member of the official’s immediate family or on (among other things): 1) any real property in
which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000)
or more; or 2) any source of income aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the
time when the decision is made.
(Section 87103(b) and (c).) A member of a city council is a
public official for purposes of the Act. (Section 82048; Teasley Advice Letter, No. 1-97-545.)
Question No. 1
You state that one of the councilmembers lives within 300 feet of the project.
8 You do
not provide a value for the councilmember’s interest in his or her residence, but we will assume
that the value is in excess of $1,000. With this assumption, we may now move to an evaluation
of whether the councilmember’s financial interest in his or her residence is impacted by the
decision to be made by the city council such that the councilmember would be disqualified from
participating in the decision under Section 87100.
Under Section 87103, the councilmember may not participate in the decision to consider
funding ofthe financial plan if the decision will have a foreseeable and material financial effect
on the councilmember’s residence.
Foreseeability
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty
is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
4
8 The fact that a person “lives” at his or her residence does not necessarily mean that the person “owns”
the residence. However, because Ms. Feierbach notes in her letter that she “owns” her residence, we will assume
for purposes of this response that when you refer to a councilmember “living” within a certain distance of the
project that the councilmember owns the residence in which he or she lives. But please note, the analysis of this
letter would change if the councilmember has a leasehold interest only in his or her residence.


File No. 1-97-577
PageS
The problem here is that we have very little facts with which to work. The information
provided in the request for advice seems to establish that the decision to be made by the city
council is the approval of fhnding for a financial plan to purchase the project properties.
However, we are not advised as to the specific mechanics of this financial plan; i.e, will funds be
raised by assessments affecting all residents ofthe city orjust residents adjacent to the project, if
special taxes are instituted, how and upon whom will they be levied, will there be any
differentiation made between residents located in or near the project and those located
elsewhere? Given that this councilmeinber’s residence is located within very close proximity to
the project, it is reasonably foreseeable that the outcome of the city’s decision on the project will
have some financial impact on the councilmember’s home even if that fmancial impact will be
leveled equally on others throughout the community.
Materiality
In order for disqualification to be required, the financial effect on the official must not
only be foreseeable, but material. Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official’s financial interest in a decision is “materially” affected as required by
Section 87103. Ifthe official’s fmancial interest is directly involved in the decision, then
Regulation 18702.1 applies to determine materiality. On the other hand, ifthe official’s financial
interest is indirectly affected by the decision, then Regulations 18702.2 to 18702.6 (depending on
the nature ofthe interest involved) apply to determine whether the effect of the decision is
material.
Because the councilmember’s residence is adjacent to, but not part of, the project, the
councilmember’s fmancial interest is indirectly involved in the decision to be rendered by the
city council concerning the financial plan.
9 Regulation 18702.3 applies to determine materiality
when a decision will indirectly affect real property owned by a public official. Hence,
Regulation 18702.3 applies in this case.
Under Regulation 1 8702.3(a)(1), the effects of a decision are presumed to be material as
to a public official’s ownership of real property if that property is located within a 300 foot
radius of the boundaries ofthe property which is subject to the decision unless evidence exists
establishing that the decision will have no financial effect on the official’s property. You have
provided us no facts which indicate that there will be an absence of financial effect on the
councilmember’s property. Therefore, by virtue ofthe fact that the councilmember’s property is
located within 300 feet ofthe project, the effect ofthe city council’s decision on the financial
If any of the factors of Regulation 18702.1 (a)(3) apply to this coundilmember’s property (e.g., the
decision to be made by the city council involves the imposition, repeal or modification of any taxes or fees assessed
or imposed on the councilmember’s property), then the city council’s decision on the fmancial plan would have a
direct effect on the councilmember’s economic interest. As such, the effect of the decision would be material and
the councilmember would be prohibited from participating in the decision.


File No. 1-97-577
Page 6
plan will be material to this councilmember. Accordingly, this councilmember may not
participate in the decision concerning the project’s financial plan.’°
Question No. 2
Foreseeability
Based on the analysis set forth under the foreseeability section ofthe response to
Question No. 1, we also conclude that a councilmember owning a home within 2,500 feet ofthe
project is more likely than not to experience some financial impact from the project through
imposition of a funding mechanism or a change in the fair market value of the home. Therefore,
it is foreseeable that the effect ofthe city council’s decision will have a financial impact on this
councilmember’s financial interest.”
Materiality
Again, because this councilmember’s residence is close to, but not part of, the project, the
councilmember’s financial interest is indirectly involved in the decision, and accordingly,
Regulation 18702.3 applies to determine materiality.
Pursuant to Regulation 18702.3(a)(3), real property in which a public official has an
interest which is located outside a radius of 300 feet but within a radius of 2,500 feet of a
proposed project will be materially affected by a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
financial effect of the decision will impact the fair market value ofthe property by $10,000 or
more or will affect the rental value of the property by $1,000 or more in a 12 month period.
Regulation 18702.3(d) states that the factors to be considered in determining these effects
include, but are not limited to:
“(1) The proximity of the property which is the subject ofthe
decision and the magnitude of the proposed project or change in
use in relationship to the property in which the official has an
interest;
(2) Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
affect the development potential or income producing potential of
the property;
(3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case of residential property,
0 We do not have sufficient information to determine if the process of funding the purchase of the project
would apply so broadly as to create an exception from disqualification under the public generally rules. (See
Regulations 18703
- 18703.3.)
We make the same assumption here that we made in response to Question No. 1, e.g., that the value of
the councilmember’s interest in his or her real property exceeds $1,000. (See Section 87103.)


File No. 1-97-577
Page 7
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will result in
a change to the character ofthe neighborhood including, but not
limited to, effect on traffic, view, privacy, intensity ofuse, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.”
We do not have any information to determine if the effects ofthe project will cause the
monetary impact required by subdivisions (a)(3)(A) and (B) of Regulation 18702.3. Therefore,
we cannot advise as to whether the impact of the city council’s decision regarding the approval
of funding for the financial plan will have a material effect on the councilmember who owns
property within the 2,500 foot radius of the project. Obviously, if you or the councilmember
conclude that the impact of the decision will have the above-referenced monetary effects, the
councilmember would be prohibited from participating in the decision.
Question No. 3
Foreseeability
If methods of funding the financial plan include the imposition of an assessment or tax on
properties located within the project area, it would be foreseeable that the effect of that
assessment or tax would be passed on to the councilmember who represents buyers and sellers of
such properties in the form of an adjustment to the councilmember’s real estate commission.
Materiality
Please recall that under Section 87103(c), a public official will be financially impacted by
a decision if the effects of the decision are material to a source of income (including a source of
promised income) to the public official)
2 Regulation 18702.6 assists in determining when a
source of income to a public official is materially affected by a decision.’
3 Regulation 18702.6
states:
“The effect of a decision is material as to an individual who is a
source of income or gifts to an official if any ofthe following
applies:
(a) The decision will affect the individual’s income, investments,
12 Section 87103(c) mandateshat the source of income represents at least $250 or more in income to the
public official in the 12 months preceding the date of the decision. You have not included facts from which we can
ascertain this threshold for evaluation; however, we will assume the threshold has been met for purposes of further
comment.
13 The test of this regulation evaluates the materiality of a decision on an individual who is a source of
income and who is indirectly affected by the decision. This test applies in this instance since the individuals who
are sources of income to the councilmember are not the subject of the decision to be made by the city council, but
their properties may be.


File No. 1-97-577
Page 8
or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or
(b) The decision will affect the individual’s real property interest in
a manner that is considered material under Section 18702.3 or
Section 18702.4.”
Again, we have not been provided with any information so as to determine materiality
under Regulation 18702.6. We, therefore, cannot assess whether the councilmember would have
a disqualifying conflict of interest. For your use, we have included copies ofRegulations
18702.3 and 18702.4.
Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Lisa L. Ditora
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:LLD:tls
Enclosures
I:\Advice Ltrs\97577.wpd

Question No. 2
Foreseeability
Based on the analysis set forth under the foreseeability section ofthe response to
Question No. 1, we also conclude that a councilmember owning a home within 2,500 feet ofthe

use in relationship to the property in which the official has an
interest;

affect the development potential or income producing potential of
the property;
(3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case of residential property,
0 We do not have sufficient information to determine if the process of funding the purchase of the project
would apply so broadly as to create an exception from disqualification under the public generally rules. (See
Regulations 18703
- 18703.3.)
We make the same assumption here that we made in response to Question No. 1, e.g., that the value of
the councilmember’s interest in his or her real property exceeds $1,000. (See Section 87103.)
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will result in
a change to the character ofthe neighborhood including, but not
limited to, effect on traffic, view, privacy, intensity ofuse, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.”
We do not have any information to determine if the effects ofthe project will cause the
monetary impact required by subdivisions (a)(3)(A) and (B) of Regulation 18702.3. Therefore,
we cannot advise as to whether the impact of the city council’s decision regarding the approval
of funding for the financial plan will have a material effect on the councilmember who owns
property within the 2,500 foot radius of the project. Obviously, if you or the councilmember
conclude that the impact of the decision will have the above-referenced monetary effects, the
councilmember would be prohibited from participating in the decision.
Question No. 3
Foreseeability
If methods of funding the financial plan include the imposition of an assessment or tax on
properties located within the project area, it would be foreseeable that the effect of that
assessment or tax would be passed on to the councilmember who represents buyers and sellers of
such properties in the form of an adjustment to the councilmember’s real estate commission.
Materiality
Please recall that under Section 87103(c), a public official will be financially impacted by
a decision if the effects of the decision are material to a source of income (including a source of
promised income) to the public official)
2 Regulation 18702.6 assists in determining when a
source of income to a public official is materially affected by a decision.’
3 Regulation 18702.6
states:
“The effect of a decision is material as to an individual who is a
source of income or gifts to an official if any ofthe following
applies:
(a) The decision will affect the individual’s income, investments,
12 Section 87103(c) mandateshat the source of income represents at least $250 or more in income to the
public official in the 12 months preceding the date of the decision. You have not included facts from which we can
ascertain this threshold for evaluation; however, we will assume the threshold has been met for purposes of further
comment.
13 The test of this regulation evaluates the materiality of a decision on an individual who is a source of
income and who is indirectly affected by the decision. This test applies in this instance since the individuals who
are sources of income to the councilmember are not the subject of the decision to be made by the city council, but
their properties may be.
or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or
(b) The decision will affect the individual’s real property interest in
a manner that is considered material under Section 18702.3 or
Section 18702.4.”
Again, we have not been provided with any information so as to determine materiality
under Regulation 18702.6. We, therefore, cannot assess whether the councilmember would have
a disqualifying conflict of interest. For your use, we have included copies ofRegulations
18702.3 and 18702.4.
Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May a councilmember who lives within 300 feet of the properties that are subject to
the advisory measure, participate in decisions to approve funding to implement the financial
plan?
5
2. May a councilmember who lives within 2,500 feet of the properties that are subject to
the advisory measure, participate in decisions to approve funding to implement the financial
plan?
3. May a councilmember who is a real estate agent and who has represented buyers and
sellers ofproperty located within the San Juan Canyon, participate in decisions to approve
funding to implement the financial plan?
information from any source to warrant a formal advice analysis. As an additional problem, Ms. Feierbach
references a Ms. Rianda in her letter and purports to seek advice on her behalf Unfortunately, we are not told who
Ms. Rianda is nor does Ms. Feierbach appear to have authority to seek advice, either formal or informal, for her.
(See Regulation 18329.)
You mention in your letter that last summer the Belmont City Council considered and declined to
approve a proposed ordinance seeking a moratorium of development of the San Juan Canyon. Because the city
council has already acted on that matter, we will not evaluate the propriety of any councilmember’s action with
respect to the matter as it constitutes past conduct under Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed). Ms. Feierbach seems to
imply in her letter that the moratorium ordinance may be resurrected. If that occurs, any affected councilmember
may request advice from this office with respect to prospective conduct to be taken in the decisionmaking process.
Generally speaking, however, any councilmember will be disqualified from participating in the consideration of the
ordinance if the decision will have a foreseeable and material impact on a fmancial interest of the coundilmember.
(Section 87103.)
You state in your letter that the reason you are contacting the FPPC is to obtain a written opinion (which
we have construed to mean a written advice letter) on behalf of certain councilmembers who must vote to “approve
the expenditure of funds to implementthe [financial] plan” required by th4assage of Measure E (the advisory
measure). We understand this to be the precise question at issue although later in your letter you appear to expand
the question to include the ability of the councilmembers to participate in “any decision which would be necessary
in order to move forward with ... the ultimate acquisition of land ....“ Because no proposal has yet been
developed to accomplish the actual acquisition of the San Juan Canyon land (apparently the completion of the
fmancial plan is preparatory to any plan of acquisition), it is impossible to evaluate whether a particular
councilmember would have a conflict of interest with respect to acquisition matters. Accordingly, we have phrased
your questions as posing the limited inquiry of whether a councilmember may participate in the vote on the
expenditure of funds for the fmancial plan.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION February 2, 1998 Jean B. Savaree City Attorney City ofBelmont 1070 Sixth Avenue Belmont, California 94002-3 893 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. 1-97-577 Dear Ms. Savaree: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Because you have not disclosed the identity of the councilmembers for whom you seek this advice, we would ordinarily refuse to provide advice of any kind. However, because you do include a letter from councihnember Coralin Feierbach and further because we believe some general advice may be instructive to you with regard to future requests, we have chosen to respond but are treating this as a request for informal assistance, under Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed). 2 As briefly mentioned above, you have included in the materials forwarded to this office a separate letter from Ms. Feierbach to the Fair Political Practices Commission (“FPPC”) seeking a response regarding conflict issues in this matter; you state in your letter that Ms. Feierbach’s letter is “part of this request for a written opinion.” Therefore, we will comply with your instruction and treat Ms. Feierbach’s letter as information supporting your request and not as a separate request for advice. 3 Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 1 8329(c)(3).) Due to the fact that you have included a letter authored by Councihnember Feierbach, we assume that she is one of the three councilmembers on whose behalf you write. However, because you have neither specifically referenced her nor any other member of the city council, we provide informal, general assistance only. (See Regulation 1 8329(c)(4)(C).) The inclusion of Ms. Feierbach’s letter does not change our decision to render informal assistance only. As will be explained under the General Conclusions portion of this letter, we have not received sufficient 428 J Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, California 95814 (916) 322-5660 File No. 1-97-577 Page 2 Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed as evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place. 4 Further, this letter is based on the facts as they have been presented to us. The Conunission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS 1.
